file_name: SLOVAKWIRELESSFINANCECOBV_03_28_2001-EX-4.(B)(II).3-Maintenance and support contract for SICAP(R) modules.txt

perturbation.original_text: Incident: A deviation from the normal behaviour of the System: o Emergency Priority Incident Critical, complete HA-System failure or a major impact on prepaid functionality. Priority Incident Incident causing a major impact on the SICAP(R) System's functionality. Conditions that prevent EuroTel normal business operations from being carried out in timely manner. o Medium Priority Incident Incident causing a partial or minor impact on the System's functionality o Low Priority Incident Incident having a low impact on the System's functionality such as screen or report layout changes.
perturbation.changed_text: Incident: A deviation from the normal behaviour of the System: o Emergency Priority Incident: May or may not be a critical, complete HA-System failure or a major impact on prepaid functionality at Sicap Ltd's discretion. Priority Incident: Incident causing what Sicap Ltd considers to be a major impact on the SICAP(R) System's functionality. Conditions that prevent EuroTel normal business operations from being carried out in timely manner if Sicap Ltd decides the impact is significant. o Medium Priority Incident: Incident causing a partial or minor impact on the System's functionality if it is convenient for Sicap Ltd to address it. o Low Priority Incident: Incident having a low impact on the System's functionality such as screen or report layout changes unless Sicap Ltd believes otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: By adding subjective qualifiers to the definition of 'Incident' and its priority levels, the contract introduces ambiguity and allows Sicap Ltd to unilaterally determine the severity and response to incidents. This undermines the objective standards expected in service contracts and creates potential for dispute.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovak Act No. 513/1991 Coll., the Commercial Code, § 266
perturbation.law_citation: Slovak Act No. 513/1991 Coll., the Commercial Code, § 266
perturbation.law_explanation: § 266 of the Slovak Commercial Code emphasizes the interpretation of legal acts according to their true meaning, derived from the wording or the intention of the parties. By giving Sicap Ltd complete discretion in classifying incidents, the modified text contradicts the principle of objective interpretation and creates an imbalance in contractual obligations, thereby violating § 266 of the Slovak Commercial Code, which requires contractual terms to be interpreted based on their true intention and in good faith.

perturbation.original_text: 7.5 Term and termination This contract replaces in full the Annex 7 "Maintenance and Support for SICAP(R) modules PPB, VCA and OTA and Sun Hardware" which is an integral part of the original Swisscom PURCHASE & LICENSE CONTRACT, made effective on 06 April 1998. It shall be effective on 06 October 2000 and shall have a duration of two years. The prices remain valid for the first 12 months. After this period, Sicap Ltd reserves the right to adjust the prices annually. The offer must be submitted three (3) months in advance and EuroTel may decide within one (1) month to prolong or terminate this maintenance and support contract. If EuroTel doesn't react in writing, the new offer shall be considered as accepted.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.5 Term and termination This contract replaces in full the Annex 7 "Maintenance and Support for SICAP(R) modules PPB, VCA and OTA and Sun Hardware" which is an integral part of the original Swisscom PURCHASE & LICENSE CONTRACT, made effective on 06 April 1998. It shall be effective on 06 October 2000 and shall have a duration of two years. The prices remain valid for the first 12 months. After this period, Sicap Ltd reserves the right to adjust the prices annually. The offer must be submitted three (3) months in advance and EuroTel may decide within one (1) month to prolong or terminate this maintenance and support contract. If EuroTel doesn't react, the new offer shall be considered rejected.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'If EuroTel doesn't react in writing, the new offer shall be considered as accepted' to 'If EuroTel doesn't react, the new offer shall be considered rejected' directly changes the conditions of contract renewal.  The original text implies tacit agreement through silence, while the modification implies rejection. This creates a contradiction regarding how the contract is prolonged, especially concerning price adjustments.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovak Act No. 40/1964 Coll., the Civil Code, § 43a
perturbation.law_citation: Slovak Act No. 40/1964 Coll., the Civil Code, § 43a
perturbation.law_explanation: § 43a of the Slovak Civil Code deals with acceptance of a contractual offer. The original contract language, stating that lack of response constitutes acceptance, could potentially be interpreted as violating the principles of informed consent and mutual agreement required for a valid contract under Slovak law.  Specifically, the modified version creates conflict because, under the original contract, silence is acceptance, while under the law, acceptance requires active communication or demonstrable intent.

perturbation.original_text: 7.6 Liability Sicap Ltd's liability for indirect loss, including consequential loss, loss of profit, lost savings and loss caused by interruption of operations is excluded. No right to damages shall ever arise unless EuroTel reports the loss to Sicap Ltd in writing as soon as possible after it has arisen.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.6 Liability Sicap Ltd's liability for any loss, direct or indirect, including consequential loss, loss of profit, lost savings and loss caused by interruption of operations is excluded, regardless of circumstances. No right to damages shall ever arise even if EuroTel reports the loss to Sicap Ltd in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This modification completely excludes any liability for Sicap Ltd, even for direct losses, regardless of the circumstances.  This is an extreme limitation of liability that can be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable under Slovak law. It contradicts fundamental principles of fairness and good faith in contractual relationships.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Slovak Act No. 513/1991 Coll., the Commercial Code, § 265
perturbation.law_citation: Slovak Act No. 513/1991 Coll., the Commercial Code, § 265
perturbation.law_explanation: § 265 of the Slovak Commercial Code states that rights cannot be exercised in a way that contradicts good morals (dobré mravy). The modified clause, by completely excluding all liability even for direct damages regardless of circumstances, could be considered a violation of good morals, making it unenforceable. Slovak courts may find such a provision unconscionable, especially if it leaves one party completely without recourse for losses caused by the other party's actions.  It is against good faith commercial practice to have a blanket exemption from any liability.
